topics.name: Classical Idea of Redox Reactions-Oxidation and Reduction Reactions
Redox Reactions in Terms of Electron Transfer Reactions

questions.question: What is the best description of the change that occurs when Na2O(s) is dissolved in water?
questions.question_raw: What is the best description of the change that occurs when Na2O(s) is dissolved in water?

options.text: Oxide ion accepts sharing in a pair of electrons.
Oxide ion donates a pair of electrons.

questions.explanation:
In water Na2O dissociates as -
Na2O + H2O → 2Na+ + O2-
The oxide ion formed can form hydrogen bonds with water and is stabilized. Thus it is easy for Na2O and all other oxides to dissolve in water as it brings about further stability in the system.

questions.question: The term oxidation was coined for the class of chemical reactions wherein one of the following options take place?
questions.question_raw: The term oxidation was coined for the class of chemical reactions wherein one of the following options take place?

options.text: Removal of the electronegative element from a substance.
Addition of hydrogen to a substance
Addition of oxygen to a substance
Addition of electropositive element to a substance.

questions.explanation:
• Oxidation means the addition of oxygen, removal of hydrogen, increase in oxidation number, the addition of an electronegative element or removal of an electropositive element.
  • ​​​​​​The term oxidation was first used to describe chemical reactions in which oxygen was added to an element of a compound. Later on, the term oxidation was extended to describe many more reactions that occurred without the use of even oxygen.
  • Addition of Oxygen: Oxidation is a chemical reaction in which oxygen is added to any chemical species (atom, ion or molecule).
  • Removal of Hydrogen: Oxidation is a chemical reaction in which hydrogen is removed from any chemical species (atom, ion or molecule).
  • Addition of an Electronegative Element: Oxidation is a chemical reaction in which an electronegative element is added into any chemical species (atom, ion or molecule).
  • Removal of an Electropositive Element: Oxidation is a chemical reaction in which an electropositive element is removed from any chemical species (atom, ion or molecule).
  • A substance that brings oxidation is known as oxidizing agents.

questions.question: Does the term reduction have one of the following options taking place?
questions.question_raw: Does the term reduction have one of the following options taking place?

options.text: Removal of the electropositive element from a substance
Addition of an electronegative element to a substance
Removal of hydrogen to a substance
Removal of oxygen from a substance

questions.explanation:
The reduction is addition of hydrogen, removal of oxygen, removal of an electronegative element or addition of an electropositive element.

  1. Removal of Oxygen: Reduction is a chemical reaction in which oxygen is removed from any chemical species (atom, ion or molecule).
    CuO + C {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Cu + CO
    H2O + C {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} CO + H2
    2CO + O2 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2CO2
    Fe3O4 + 4H2 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 3F2 + 4H2O
  2. Addition of Hydrogen: Reduction is a chemical reaction in which hydrogen is added to any chemical species (atom, ion or molecule).
    Cl2 + H2 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2HCl
    S + H2 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} H2S
    C2H4 + H2 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} C2H6
  3. Removal of an Electronegative Element: Reduction is a chemical reaction in which an electronegative element is removed from any chemical species (atom, ion or molecule).
  4. 2HgCl2 + SnCl2 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Hg2Cl2 + SnCl4 (Reduction of mercuric chloride)
  5. 2FeCl3 + H2 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2FeCl2 + 2HCl (Reduction of ferric chloride)
  6. 2FeCl3 + H2S {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2FeCl2 + 2HCl + S (Reduction of ferric chloride)
  7. Addition of an Electropositive Element: Reduction is a chemical reaction in which an electropositive element is added any chemical species (atom, ion or molecule).
    HgCl2 + Hg {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Hg2Cl2 (Reduction of mercuric chloride)
    CuCl2 + Cu {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Cu2Cl2 (Reduction of cupric chloride)
  8. The substance which brings reduction is known as a reducing agent.
  9. A substance, which undergoes oxidation, acts as a reducing agent while a substance, which undergoes reduction, acts as an oxidizing agent.

Mg, S, Cu, Na2SO3, H2S, HI, H2, C, KI are reducing agents, while O2, Cl2, F2, H2O2, MnO2, FeCl3, CuCl2, Fe3O4, CuO, etc., are oxidizing agents in the above examples.

  1. All oxidation and reduction reactions are complimentary of one another and occur simultaneously, one cannot take place without the other.
  2. No single oxidation and no single reduction process is known. The simultaneously oxidation and reduction reactions are generally termed as redox reactions. e.g., 2FeCl3 + SnCl2 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2FeCl2 + SnCl4.

In above example iron undergoes reduction from +3 to +2.

questions.question:
The formation of sodium chloride involves two half-reactions, which clearly shows the involvement of electrons.
2Na (s) $\longrightarrow$ 2Na + (g) + 2e
Cl2 (g) + 2e $\longrightarrow$ 2 Cl(g)
Sum of the half reactions gives
2 Na(s) + Cl2(g) $\longrightarrow$ 2 Na + Cl (s) or 2 NaCl (s)
In terms of electron-transfer change, name the oxidising agent(s) and reducing agent(s).
questions.question_raw:
The formation of sodium chloride involves two half-reactions, which clearly shows the involvement of electrons.
2Na (s) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2Na + (g) + 2e
Cl2 (g) + 2e  {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex}  2 Cl(g)
Sum of the half reactions gives
2 Na(s) + Cl2(g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex}  2 Na + Cl (s) or 2 NaCl (s)
In terms of electron-transfer change, name the oxidising agent(s) and reducing agent(s).

options.text: Oxidising agent: Chlorine and Reducing agent: Sodium
Reducing agents: Chlorine, oxygen and sulphur and oxidising agent: sodium
Oxidising agents: Chlorine, oxygen and reducing agents: sodium and sulphur
Reducing agents: Chlorine, oxygen and oxidising agents: sodium and sulphur

questions.explanation:
According to the modern concept, the loss of electrons is oxidation whereas the gain of electrons is reduction. Redox reaction involves two half-reactions, one involving loss of electron or electrons (oxidation) and the other involving gain of electrons or electrons (reduction)

  1. Oxidizing agent is a species that can gain one or more electrons.
  2. Reducing agent is a species which can lose one or more electrons.

In the given reaction Sodium(Na) is losing 2 electrons, thus it is the reducing agent. Chlorine(Cl) is gaining 2 electrons, thus it is an oxidising agent.

questions.question: Copper nitrate is a blue coloured solution. Place a strip of metallic zinc in an aqueous solution of copper nitrate for about one hour. What happens?
questions.question_raw: Copper nitrate is a blue coloured solution. Place a strip of metallic zinc in an aqueous solution of copper nitrate for about one hour. What happens?

options.text: The blue colour of the solution turns to red
The zinc strip becomes coated with blue colour
The aqueous solution of copper nitrate turns green in colour and the metallic zinc strip turns darker in colour.
The blue colour of the solution becomes more intense blue.

questions.explanation:
On placing a strip of metallic zinc in an aqueous solution of copper nitrate for about one hour, the copper nitrate solution turns green in colour and zinc strip turns darker.
Formula and Ionic equation for the reaction are as follows.

Formula Equation: Zn (s) + Cu(NO3)2 (aq) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Cu (s) + Zn(NO3)2 (aq)
Ionic Equation: Zn (s) + Cu2+ (aq) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Zn2+ (aq) + Cu (s)

As is seen in ionic equation Zn is losing 2 electrons, thus oxidation occurs and Cu is gaining 2 electrons, thus reduction occurs. Since oxidation and reduction take place simultaneously in this reaction, it is a redox reaction. Also since Zn is losing electrons and becoming darker in colour, it is the reducing agent and Cu is gaining 2 electrons, it is the reducing agent. The resultant solution Zinc nitrate [Zn(NO3)2] is green in colour.

questions.question: Redox reaction between copper and an aqueous solution of silver nitrate happens when the copper rod is dipped in silver nitrate solution. What happens in the final stage?
questions.question_raw: Redox reaction between copper and an aqueous solution of silver nitrate happens when the copper rod is dipped in silver nitrate solution. What happens in the final stage?

options.text: Silver deposits in the solution; Ag+(aq) is reduced to Ag(s).
Copper deposits in the solution; Cu(aq) is reduced to ${\text{Cu}}_2^ + $ (s).
Silver deposits on the rod; Ag+(aq) is oxidised to Ag(s).
The solution turns intense blue, and silver is deposited on the copper rod.

questions.explanation:
This is due to the formation of Cu2+  ions in the solution illustrated in the following ionic reaction:
Cu(S) + 2Ag+(aq)  {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Cu2+(aq) + 2Ag(s)
Here, Cu is oxidized to Cu2+ (means releasing of 2 electrons from Cu to Cu2+ ) and Ag+ is reduced to Ag(s) (means gaining of 2 electrons from 2Ag+ to 2Ag ) and thus, silver converted into a solid-state and deposited on a copper rod.

questions.question: Oxidation number denotes the oxidation state of an element in a compound ascertained on the basis that electron in a covalent bond belongs
questions.question_raw: Oxidation number denotes the oxidation state of an element in a compound ascertained on the basis that electron in a covalent bond belongs

options.text: entirely to halogens in a compound
entirely to transition elements if present

questions.explanation:
Oxidation number is a number assigned to an element in a compound according to some rules. This number enable us to describe oxidation-reduction reactions, and balancing redox chemical reactions. When a covalent bond forms between two atoms with different electronegativities the shared electrons in the bond lie closer to the more electronegative atom eg. HCl

The oxidation number of an atom is the charge that results when the electrons in a covalent bond are assigned to the more electronegative atom. It is the charge on an atom would possess if the bonding were ionic. In HCl (above) the oxidation number for the hydrogen would be +1 and that of the Cl would be -1

questions.question: Displacement of hydrogen from cold water is done by:
questions.question_raw: Displacement of hydrogen from cold water is done by:
questions.explanation:
A common characteristic of most Alkali metal is their ability to displace H2 (g) from water. This is represented by their large, negative electrode potentials. In this event, the Group 1 metal is oxidized to its metal ion, and water is reduced to form hydrogen gas and hydroxide ions. The general reaction of an alkali metal (M) with H2O (l) is given in the following equation:
2M(s) + 2H2O(l) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2M + (aq) + 2OH−(aq) + H2(g)
From this reaction it is apparent that OH- is produced, creating a basic or alkaline environment. Group 1 elements are called alkali metals because of their ability to displace H2 (g) from water and create a basic solution. Alkali metals are also known to react violently and explosively with water. This is because enough heat is given off during the exothermic reaction to ignite the H2(g).

questions.question: Fractional Oxidation Number exists because:
questions.question_raw: Fractional Oxidation Number exists because:

options.text: electrons are never shared/transferred in that element.
the average oxidation state of the element is divided into two.
the element is present in different oxidation states.
middle carbon in such elements has always zero oxidation state.

questions.explanation:
The oxidation states are usually in whole numbers, but in some cases, they are in fractional numbers. Consider Fe3O4.

  • Using rule #2, the sum of the oxidation state of all atoms is 0
  • Using rule #6, the O has the oxidation state of -2. 4 {tex}\times{/tex} (-2) = -8
  • Equation: 3Fe + (-8) = 0
  • 3Fe = 8
  • Fe = {tex}8 \over 3{/tex}

In most cases, atoms of the same element in a given compound have the same oxidation states, but each atom can have a different state than the other. For Fe3O4, two Fe atoms have an oxidation state of +3 and one of +2, which makes the total oxidation state of Fe= {tex}8 \over 3{/tex}.
Instead of averaging the oxidation states, we must keep them separated. This is called fragmenting, which occurs if there is an ionic compound, and the ions can be separated. The oxidation state of the individual atoms in the different ions can be determined.

questions.question: Assign oxidation number to P in NaH2PO4.
questions.question_raw: Assign oxidation number to P in NaH2PO4.
questions.explanation:
The oxidation state of P in NaH2PO4 is +5.
Let the oxidation number of P be x. As we already know the oxidation number of other atoms so we can calculate that of P too.
Na = +1
H= +1 (+2 for hydrogen molecule)
O = -2
Now calculating oxidation number of P:-
1 +2 + x +(-8) = 0
On solving we get x = +5.

questions.question: Assign oxidation number to S in KAl(SO4)2.12H2O:
questions.question_raw: Assign oxidation number to S in KAl(SO4)2.12H2O:
questions.explanation:
Oxidation state of S in KAl(SO4)2.12H2O is +6.
As, Oxidation number of S in KAl(SO4)2.12 H2O can be calculated as-
1 + 3 + 2X - 16   +  0 = 0  (H2O is neutral so oxidation No. is 0)
2X = 12
X = +6

questions.question: If an excess of P4 is treated with F2, then_____will be produced wherein the oxidation number of P is +3. The compound is________.
questions.question_raw: If an excess of P4 is treated with F2, then_____will be produced wherein the oxidation number of P is +3. The compound is________.
questions.explanation:
Whenever a reaction between an oxidising agent and a reducing agent is carried out, a compound of lower oxidation state is formed if the reducing agent is in excess and a compound of higher oxidation state is formed if the oxidising agent is in excess. This can be illustrated as follows: P4 and F2 are reducing and oxidising agents respectively. If an excess of P4 is treated with F2, then PF3 will be produced, wherein the oxidation number (O.N.) of P is +3.
PF4(excess)+F2 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} PF3

questions.question: Which of the following is not an example of redox reaction?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following is not an example of redox reaction?
questions.explanation:
The reaction is not redox because there is no oxidation or reduction of any element (i.e. no change in oxidation state/number).
Ba in BaCl2 is at a +2 charge because it is in group 2. On the other side of the equation Ba in BaSO4 is still +2. Cl2 on the first side of the equation is -2 (as 2 chlorines) and Cl is -2 on the other side as there are 2 of them.
H on both sides are both +1 (but technically +2 as there are two of them).
SO4 does not change oxidation number and always remains -2.
So, the reaction is not a redox reaction.

2 Na(s) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2Na+ + 2e-

options.text: Sodium is oxidised and hydrogen is reduced
Electronegativity of sodium determines the direction of the reaction

questions.explanation:
Oxidation is a process in which one or more electrons are lost and the oxidation number is increased. Sodium has 0 oxidation state as a reactant and +1 oxidation state as a product.
Similarly, Reduction is a process in which one or more electrons are gained and the oxidation number is reduced i.e. from +1 to 0.

questions.explanation: By definition, the oxidation number of an atom is the charge that an atom would have if the compound was composed of ions. The oxidation number of simple ions is equal to the charge on the ion. The oxidation number of sodium in the Na+ ion is +1, for example, and the oxidation number of chlorine in the Cl- ion is -1.

The more positive the value of {tex}E^{\ominus}{/tex}, the greater is the tendency of the species to get reduced. Using the standard electrode potential of redox couples given below to find out which of the following is the strongest oxidising agent.
{tex}E^{\ominus}{/tex} values: {tex}\frac{{{\text{F}}{{\text{e}}^{{\text{3 + }}}}}}{{{\text{F}}{{\text{e}}^{{\text{2 + }}}}}}{/tex} = +0.77; {tex}\frac{{{{\text{I}}^{\text{2}}}{\text{(s)}}}}{{{{\text{I}}^{\text{ - }}}}}{/tex} = +0.54;
{tex}\frac{{{\text{C}}{{\text{u}}^{{\text{2 + }}}}}}{{{\text{Cu}}}}{/tex} = +0.34; {tex}\frac{{{\text{A}}{{\text{g}}^{\text{ + }}}}}{{{\text{Ag}}}}{/tex}= +0.80 V

questions.question_raw:
Using the standard electrode potential, find out the pair between which redox reactions is not feasible.
{tex}E^{\ominus}{/tex} values:  {tex}\frac{{{\text{F}}{{\text{e}}^{{\text{3 + }}}}}}{{{\text{F}}{{\text{e}}^{{\text{2 + }}}}}}{/tex} = +0.77; {tex}\frac{{{{\text{I}}^{\text{2}}}}}{{{{\text{I}}^{\text{ - }}}}}{/tex}(s) = +0.54;
{tex}\frac{{{\text{C}}{{\text{u}}^{{\text{2 + }}}}}}{{{\text{Cu}}}}{/tex} = +0.34; {tex}\frac{{{\text{A}}{{\text{g}}^{\text{ + }}}}}{{{\text{Ag}}}}{/tex} = +0.80V
questions.explanation:
2Ag {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2Ag+1 + 2e- {tex}E^{\ominus}{/tex} =- 0.80 V
2Fe3+ + 2e- {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2Fe+2 {tex}E^{\ominus}{/tex} = +0.77 V
On adding the values we get,
{tex}E^{\ominus}{/tex}cell = -0.03 V
{tex}E^{\ominus}{/tex} a cell is an electromotive force (also called cell voltage or cell potential) between two half-cells. The greater the
{tex}E^{\ominus}{/tex} a cell of a reaction the greater the driving force of electrons through the system, the more likely the reaction will proceed.
Thus, the reaction will not proceed as standard cell potential is less than zero.

questions.question: The oxidizing power of halogens increase in the order of:
questions.question_raw: The oxidizing power of halogens increase in the order of:
questions.explanation:
Halogens have high electronegativity and electron affinity. They have a greater tendency to accept electrons or easily reduced, therefore they are a strong oxidizing agent.
As the reduction potential decrease down the group, the oxidizing power decrease down the group the order of the oxidizing power will be as under
F2 > Cl2> Br2> I2
The oxidizing power depends on,
The heat of dissociation of halogen molecule.
Electron affinity of the atom.
Hydration energy of the ion.
Heat of vaporization
If a halogen has low energy of dissociation, a high electron affinity, and higher hydration of its ion, it will have high oxidizing power.
F has although low electron affinity than Cl but low dissociation energy and has high hydration energy of its ion, therefore Fluorine is the strongest oxidizing agent.

questions.question: A complex compound in which the oxidation number of metal is zero is:
questions.question_raw: A complex compound in which the oxidation number of metal is zero is:
questions.explanation:
The oxidation number is the charge left on the central atom when all the bonding pairs of electrons are removed, with the charge devolved to the most electronegative atom.
For Ni(CO)4, we break the Ni−CO bond and we get {tex}Ni^0 +4×^−: C≡ O^+. {/tex} And thus nickel has a formal oxidation state of 0.
Because Ni(CO)4 is quite volatile, its formation forms the basis for the Mond process for nickel purification.

Kc = antilog 20 = 1020
Kc = 1020

questions.question: In which of the following compounds, an element exhibits two different oxidation states?

questions.explanation:
NH4NO3 is an ionic compound consisting of NH4+ and NO3- ion. The oxidation number of N in two species is different as shown below:
In the case of NH4+,
Let the oxidation state of N in NH4+ be x.
x + 4 {tex}\times{/tex} (+1) = +1
x = -3
In the NO3-,
Let the oxidation state of N in NO3-  be y,
y + 3 {tex}\times{/tex} (-2) = -1
y = +5
Hence, NH4NO3 exhibits two different oxidation states.

questions.question: In Ostwald’s process for the manufacture of nitric acid, the first step involves the oxidation of ammonia gas by oxygen gas to give nitric oxide gas and steam. What is the maximum weight of nitric oxide that can be obtained starting only with 10.00 g of ammonia and 20.00 g of oxygen?
questions.question_raw: In Ostwald’s process for the manufacture of nitric acid, the first step involves the oxidation of ammonia gas by oxygen gas to give nitric oxide gas and steam. What is the maximum weight of nitric oxide that can be obtained starting only with 10.00 g of ammonia and 20.00 g of oxygen?
questions.explanation:
The reaction that takes place in ammonia and oxygen is given below:
{tex}\mathop {4N{H_3}}\limits_{68g} + \mathop {5{O_2}}\limits_{160g} \to \mathop {4NO}\limits_{120g} + 6{H_2}O{/tex}
Limiting reagent
160g of oxygen reacts with 68g of ammonia
20g of oxygen reacts with {tex}\frac{68\times20}{160} {/tex} = 8.5g of ammonia
Therefore for 20g of oxygen 8.5g of ammonia is used. Therefore 1.5g ammonia is in excess and therefore oxygen is the limiting reagent.
160 g of O2 produces = 120g of NO 
Therefore, 20 g of O2 produces= {tex}\frac{120\times20}{60}{/tex} = 15 g of NO.

questions.question:
On the reaction 
2Ag + 2H2SO4 $\longrightarrow$ Ag2SO4 + 2H2O + SO2 sulphuric acid acts as an?
questions.question_raw:
On the reaction 
2Ag + 2H2SO4 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Ag2SO4 + 2H2O + SO2 sulphuric acid acts as an?

options.text: an acid as well as an oxidant

questions.explanation:
sulfuric acid (H2SO4) is a strong acid, signifying that it fully dissociates into H3O+ and HSO4- in an aqueous environment (the bisulfate ion is amphiprotic,  but usually behaved as a weak bronsted acid given it's feeble alkalinity).
Now, consider some oxidizing agents: F2, O2, Cl2, etc. All of these species are driven by high electronegativities/electron affinities, a result of quantum mechanical effects that contribute to the properties of these agents. However, the H+ (H3O+) ion is essentially a naked proton, resulting in an extremely dense positive charge; this will force it's reduction by more electropositive species by either physical gaining of electrons or the sharing of a lone pair.
Furthermore, the bisulfate/sulfate ion is capable of forming entropically preferable compounds due to the presence of oxygen, another powerful oxidizing agent.

questions.question:
Given,
Co3+ + e- $\rightarrow$ Co2+ ; E° = +1.81 V
Pb4+ + 2e- $\rightarrow$ Pb2+ ; E° = +1.67 V
Ce4+ + e- $\rightarrow$ Ce3+ ; E° = +1.61 V
Bi3+ + 3e- $\rightarrow$ Bi; E° = +0.20 V
Oxidising power of the species will increase in the order
questions.question_raw:
Given,
Co3+ + e- {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Co2+ ; E° = +1.81 V
Pb4+ + 2e- {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Pb2+ ; E° = +1.67 V
Ce4+ + e- {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Ce3+ ; E° = +1.61 V
Bi3+ + 3e- {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Bi; E° = +0.20 V
Oxidising power of the species will increase in the order
questions.explanation:
Given, Co3+ + e- {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Co2+ ; E° = +1.81 V
Pb4+ + 2e- {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Pb2+ ; E° = +1.67 V
Ce4+ + e- {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Ce3+ ; E° = +1.61 V
Bi3+ + 3e- {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Bi; E° = +0.20 V
Oxidising power of the species increases in the order of Bi3+ < Ce4+ < Pb4+ < Co3+
Higher the emf value, stronger the oxidising power. The maximum value of emf is possessed by Co3+. Hence, it has maximum oxidising power. Whereas Bi3+ possess the lowest emf value. Hence, it has minimum oxidising power.

questions.question: A solution of Ni(NO3)2 is electrolyzed between platinum electrodes using 0.1 Faraday electricity. How many moles of Ni will be deposited at the cathode?
questions.question_raw: A solution of Ni(NO3)2 is electrolyzed between platinum electrodes using 0.1 Faraday electricity. How many moles of Ni will be deposited at the cathode?
questions.explanation:
A solution of Ni(NO3)2 is electrolysed between platinum electrodes using 0.1 Faraday electricity. It means that 0.1 equivalent of Ni2+ will be discharged. Electrolysis of Ni(NO3)2 gives Ni2+ + 2e- {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Ni (Atomic mass of Ni = 58.7)
Number of equivalents = Number of moles {tex}\times{/tex} number of electrons.
0.1 = Number of moles {tex}\times{/tex} 2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Number of moles Ni = {tex}\frac{0.1}{2}{/tex} = 0.05

questions.question:
Calculate the standard cell potential (in V) of the cell in which following reaction takes place
Fe2+ (aq) + Ag+(aq) $\rightarrow$ Fe3+(aq ) + Ag(s)
Given that,
$E_{\mathrm{Ag}^{+} / \mathrm{Ag}}^{\circ}=x \mathrm{V}$
$E^{\circ}_{\mathrm{Fe}^{2+} / \mathrm{Fe}}=y \mathrm{V}$
$E^{\circ}_{\mathrm{Fe}^{3+} / \mathrm{Fe}}=z \mathrm{V}$
questions.question_raw:
Calculate the standard cell potential (in V) of the cell in which following reaction takes place
Fe2+ (aq) + Ag+(aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Fe3+(aq ) + Ag(s)
Given that,
{tex}E_{\mathrm{Ag}^{+} / \mathrm{Ag}}^{\circ}=x \mathrm{V}{/tex}
{tex}E^{\circ}_{\mathrm{Fe}^{2+} / \mathrm{Fe}}=y \mathrm{V}{/tex}
{tex}E^{\circ}_{\mathrm{Fe}^{3+} / \mathrm{Fe}}=z \mathrm{V}{/tex}
questions.explanation:
Fe2+ (aq) + Ag+ (aq) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Fe3+ (aq) + Ag(s)
{tex}E_{\text {cell }}^{o}=E_{\mathrm{Ag}^{+} / \mathrm{Ag}}^{\circ}-E_{\mathrm{Fe}^{3+} / \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}}^{\circ}=x \mathrm{V}-E_{\mathrm{Fe}^{3+} / \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}}^{\circ}{/tex} ...(i)
Now, for two half-cells

  1. {tex}\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}+2 e^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Fe} ; E_{\mathrm{Fe}^{2+} / \mathrm{Fe}}^{\circ}=E_{1}^{\circ}=y \mathrm{V} \Delta G_{2}^{\circ}=-2 F E_{1}^{o}{/tex}
  2. {tex}\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}+3 e^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Fe} ; E_{\mathrm{Fe}^{3+} / \mathrm{Fe}}^{\circ}=E_{2}^{\circ}=z \mathrm{V} \Delta G_{2}^{0}=-3 \mathrm{FE}_{2}^{\circ}{/tex}

So, {tex}\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}+e^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Fe}^{2+} ; E_{\mathrm{Fe}^{3+} / \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}}^{\circ}=E_{3}^{\circ}=?{/tex}
{tex}\Delta G_{3}^{\circ}=-1 \times F E_{3}^{\circ}{/tex}
Again, {tex}\Delta G_{3}^{\circ}=\Delta G_{2}^{\circ}-\Delta G_{1}^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad-F E_{3}^{\circ}=-3 F E_{2}^{\circ}-\left(-2 F E_{1}^{\circ}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad-E_{3}^{\circ}=2 E_{1}^{\circ}-3 E_{2}^{\circ} \Rightarrow E_{3}^{\circ}=3 E_{2}^{\circ}-2 E_{1}^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow E_{Fe^{3+/Fe^{2+}}}^o{/tex}= (3z - 2y) V
So, from equation (i)
{tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}{/tex} = xV - (3z - 2y) V = (x - 3z + 2y) V

questions.question_raw:
Given, that {tex}E_{\mathrm{O}_{2} / \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}^{\ominus}=+1.23 \mathrm{V}{/tex};
{tex}E_{\mathrm{S}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{8}^{2-} / \mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}}^{\ominus}=2.05 \mathrm{V}{/tex};
{tex}E_{\mathrm{Br}_{2} / \mathrm{Br}^{-}}^{\ominus}=+1.09 \mathrm{V}{/tex};
{tex}E_{\mathrm{Au}^{3+} / \mathrm{Au}}^{\ominus}=+1.4 \mathrm{V}{/tex}
The strongest oxidising agent is
questions.explanation:
Higher the standard reduction potential {tex}\left(E^{\circ}_{M^{n+} / M}\right){/tex}, better is oxidising agent. Among the given, {tex}E_{\mathrm{S}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{1}^{2-} / \mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}}^{\circ}{/tex}is highest, hence {tex}\mathrm{S}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{8}^{2-}{/tex} is the strongest oxidising agent.
The decreasing order of oxidising agent among the given option is as follows: {tex}\mathrm{S}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{8}^{2-}{/tex} > Au3+ > O2 > Br2

questions.question: The anodic half-cell of lead-acid battery is recharged using electricity of 0.05 Faraday. The amount of PbSO4 electrolysed in g during the process is (Molar mass of PbSO4 = 303g mol-1)
questions.question_raw: The anodic half-cell of lead-acid battery is recharged using electricity of 0.05 Faraday. The amount of PbSO4 electrolysed in g during the process is (Molar mass of PbSO4 = 303g mol-1)
questions.explanation:
During charging: {tex}\mathrm{Pb}+\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{PbSO}_{4}+2 e^{-}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1 F {tex}\equiv{/tex} 1 g - equiv. of PbSO4
{tex}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}mol of PbSO4 {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{303}{2}{/tex}g PbSO4
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 0.05 F {tex}\equiv\frac{303}{2} \times 0.05{/tex}g of PbSO4
= 7.575 g of PbSO4

questions.question: How long (approximate) should water be electrolysed by passing through 100 amperes current so that the oxygen released can completely burn 27.66 g of diborane? (Atomic weight of B = 10.8 $\mu$)
questions.question_raw: How long (approximate) should water be electrolysed by passing through 100 amperes current so that the oxygen released can completely burn 27.66 g of diborane? (Atomic weight of B = 10.8 {tex}\mu{/tex})
questions.explanation:
Given that, i = 100 amp. also, 27.66 g of diborane (B2H6)
Molecular mass of B2H6 = 10.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 + 6 = 27.6
Number of moles of B2H6 in 27.66 g = {tex}\frac{\text { Given mass }}{\text { Molar mass }}=\frac{27.66}{27.6} \approx 1{/tex}
Now consider the equation B2H6 + 3O2 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} B2O3 + 3H2O
From the equation we can interpret that 3 moles of oxygen is required to bum 1 mole (i.e. 27.6 g) B2H6 completely.
Also consider the electrolysis reaction of water i.e.
{tex}\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{O}^{--}{/tex}
{tex}2 \mathrm{H}^{+} \frac{+2 e^{-}}{\text {Cathode }} 2 \mathrm{H} \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}_{2} \uparrow{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{O}^{--} \frac{\text { Anode }}{-2 \mathrm{e}^{-}}, O \frac{2 \text { such }}{\text { atoms }}, \mathrm{O}_{2} \uparrow{/tex}
From the above equation it can be easily interpreted that in electrolysis of water for the production of 1 mole of oxygen from 1 mole of H2O at anode 4 moles electrons are required. Likewise for the production of 3 moles of O2 12(3 {tex}\times{/tex} 4) moles of electrons will be needed.
So, the total amount of charge required to produce 3 moles of oxygen will be 12 {tex}\times{/tex} F or 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 96500
We know Q = it
So, 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 96500 = 100 {tex}\times{/tex} t in seconds
or {tex}\frac{12 \times 96500}{100 \times 3600}{/tex} = t in hours = 3.2 hours

questions.question:
Given, $E_{\mathrm{Cl}_{2} / \mathrm{Cl}^{-}}^{\circ}=1.36 \mathrm{V}, E_{\mathrm{Cr}^{3+} / \mathrm{Cr}}^{\circ}$ = - 0.74 V
$E_{\mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}^{2-} / \mathrm{Cr}^{3+}}^{\circ}=1.33 \mathrm{V}, E_{\mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{-} / \mathrm{Mn}^{2+}}^{\circ}$ = 1.51 V
Among the following, the strongest reducing agent is
questions.question_raw:
Given, {tex}E_{\mathrm{Cl}_{2} / \mathrm{Cl}^{-}}^{\circ}=1.36 \mathrm{V}, E_{\mathrm{Cr}^{3+} / \mathrm{Cr}}^{\circ}{/tex} = - 0.74 V
{tex}E_{\mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}^{2-} / \mathrm{Cr}^{3+}}^{\circ}=1.33 \mathrm{V}, E_{\mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{-} / \mathrm{Mn}^{2+}}^{\circ}{/tex} = 1.51 V
Among the following, the strongest reducing agent is
questions.explanation: The substances which have lower reduction potentials are stronger reducing agents. Therefore, Cr {tex}\left(E_{\mathrm{Cr}^{3+} / \mathrm{Cr}}^{\circ}=-0.74 \mathrm{V}\right){/tex} is the strongest reducing agent among all the other given options.

questions.question:
For the following electrochemical cell at 298 K,
Pt(s)|H2(g), (1bar)|H+(aq),(1M)||M4+(aq),M2+(aq)|Pt(s)
$E_{\mathrm{cell}}=0.092 \mathrm{V} \text { when } \frac{\left[M^{2+}(a q)\right]}{\left[M^{4+}(a q)\right]}=10^{x}$
Given : $E_{M^{4+} / M^{2+}}^{\circ}=0.151 \mathrm{V} ; 2.303 \frac{R T}{F}=0.059 \mathrm{V}$
The value of x is
questions.question_raw:
For the following electrochemical cell at 298 K,
Pt(s)|H2(g), (1bar)|H+(aq),(1M)||M4+(aq),M2+(aq)|Pt(s)
{tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}=0.092 \mathrm{V} \text { when } \frac{\left[M^{2+}(a q)\right]}{\left[M^{4+}(a q)\right]}=10^{x}{/tex}
Given : {tex}E_{M^{4+} / M^{2+}}^{\circ}=0.151 \mathrm{V} ; 2.303 \frac{R T}{F}=0.059 \mathrm{V}{/tex}
The value of x is
questions.explanation:
Oxidation at anode
{tex}\mathrm{H}_{2}(g) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{H}^{+}(a q)+2 e^{-} ; \quad E_{\mathrm{SHE}}^{\circ}{/tex} = 0.00 V
Reduction at cathode
{tex}M^{4+}(a q)+2e^- \longrightarrow M^{2+}(a q) ; E_{M^{4+} / M^{2}}^{\circ}{/tex} = 0.151 V
Net: M4+ (aq) + H2(g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} M2+ (aq) + 2H+ (aq);
{tex}K=\frac{\left[M^{2+}\right]\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]^{2}}{\left[M^{4+}\right] p_{\mathrm{H}_{2}}}\left(E_{\mathrm{ell}}^{\circ}=0.151 \mathrm{V}\right)=\frac{\left[M^{2+}\right]}{\left[M^{4+}\right]}{/tex}
{tex}E_{\text {cell }}=E_{\text {rell }}^{\circ}-\frac{0.059}{2}{/tex} logK
0.092 = 0.151 {tex}-\frac{0.059}{2} \log \frac{\left[M^{2+}\right]}{\left[M^{4+}\right]}{/tex}
0.059 = {tex}\frac{0.059}{2}{/tex} log 10x
{tex}\therefore{/tex} log 10x = 2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x = 2

questions.question: Two Faraday of electricity is passed through a solution of CuSO4. The mass of copper deposited at the cathode is (at. mass of Cu = 63.5 u)
questions.question_raw: Two Faraday of electricity is passed through a solution of CuSO4. The mass of copper deposited at the cathode is (at. mass of Cu = 63.5 u)
questions.explanation:
Given, Q = 2F
Atomic mass of Cu = 63.5u
Valency of the metal Z = 2
We have, CuSO4 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Cu2+ + {tex}\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}{/tex}
{tex}\underset{1mol}{Cu^{2+}}+\;\underset{\underset{2F}{2mol}}{2e^-}\longrightarrow\underset{1mol=63.5g}{Cu}{/tex}
Alternatively
W = ZQ = {tex}\frac{E}{F}{/tex} {tex}\cdot{/tex} 2F = 2E = {tex}\frac{2 \times 63.5}{2}{/tex} = 63.5

questions.question: Electrolysis of dilute aqueous NaCl solution was carried out by passing 10 mA current. The time required to liberate 0.01 mole of H2 gas at the cathode is (1 F = 96500 C mol-1)
questions.question_raw: Electrolysis of dilute aqueous NaCl solution was carried out by passing 10 mA current. The time required to liberate 0.01 mole of H2 gas at the cathode is (1 F = 96500 C mol-1)
questions.explanation:
0.01 mol of H2 = 0.02 g equivalent
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}Coulombs required = 0.02 {tex}\times{/tex} 96500 = 1930 C
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}Q = It = 1930 C
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} t = {tex}\frac{1930}{10 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} = 19.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 104 s

questions.question: In the electrolytic cell, flow of electrons is from
questions.question_raw: In the electrolytic cell, flow of electrons is from

options.text: Cathode to anode through internal supply
Anode to cathode through internal supply

questions.explanation:
In electrolytic cell electrolysis occur at the cost of electricity :
At cathode : Mn+ + ne {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} M (electron gone in solution)
At anode: Xn- {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} X + ne- (electron supplied to anode)
Therefore, electron is moving from cathode to anode via internal circuit.

options.text: $\mathrm{Cr_2O}_{7}^{2-}$ can be used in aqueous HCI
$\mathrm{Cr_2O}_{7}^{2-}$ can be used in aqueous H2SO4
$\mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{-}$ can be used in aqueous HCI
$\mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{-}$ can be used in aqueous H2SO4

questions.explanation:
{tex}\mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{-}{/tex} cannot be used for oxidation of Fe2+ in HCI medium because the following reaction is spontaneous:
{tex}\mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{-}{/tex} + Cl- {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex}Mn2+ + Cl2; Eo = 1.51 - 1.40 = 0.11 V
In all other cases, the redox process between oxidising agent and medium (HCl or H2SO4) are non-spontaneous, would not interfere oxidation of Fe2+

questions.question: Saturated solution of KNO3 is used to make salt-bridge because
questions.question_raw: Saturated solution of KNO3 is used to make salt-bridge because

options.text: Velocities of both K+ and $NO_3^-$ are nearly the same
KNO3 is highly soluble in water
Velocity of $NO_3^-$ is greater than that of K+
Velocity of K+ is greater than that of $NO_3^-$

questions.explanation:
A saturated KNO₃ solution is used in salt bridges because:

  1. K⁺ and NO₃⁻ ions have nearly equal mobilities (velocities) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Minimizes junction potential.
  2. Highly soluble {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Ensures good conductivity.
  3. Chemically inert {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Doesn’t interfere with cell reactions.

questions.question:
A solution containing one mole per litre of each Cu (NO3)2, AgNO3, Hg2 (NO3)2 and Mg(NO3)2 is being electrolysed by using inert electrodes. The values of standard electrode potentials in volts (reduction potential) are
Ag+/Ag = + 0.80, $\mathrm{Hg}_{2}^{2+}$/2Hg = +0.79
Cu2+/Cu = + 0.34, Mg2+/Mg = - 2.37
With increasing voltage, the sequence of deposition of metals on the cathode will be
questions.question_raw:
A solution containing one mole per litre of each Cu (NO3)2, AgNO3, Hg2 (NO3)2 and Mg(NO3)2 is being electrolysed by using inert electrodes. The values of standard electrode potentials in volts (reduction potential) are
Ag+/Ag = + 0.80, {tex}\mathrm{Hg}_{2}^{2+}{/tex}/2Hg = +0.79
Cu2+/Cu = + 0.34, Mg2+/Mg = - 2.37
With increasing voltage, the sequence of deposition of metals on the cathode will be
questions.explanation:
In aqueous solution, only those ions who are less electropositive than hydrogen (E° > 0) would be deposited. Therefore, in the present case, only Ag, Hg and Cu would be deposited on passing electricity through aqueous solution of these ions, Mg will not be deposited.
Also, higher the value of E°, easier will be their reduction, therefore, the sequence in which ions will be deposited on increasing voltage across the electrodes is : Ag, Hg, Cu

questions.question:
The decreasing order of electrical conductivity of the following aqueous solution is
0.1 M formic acid (.A),
0.1 M acetic acid (B),
0.1 M benzoic acid (C)
questions.question_raw:
The decreasing order of electrical conductivity of the following aqueous solution is
0.1 M formic acid (.A),
0.1 M acetic acid (B),
0.1 M benzoic acid (C)
questions.explanation:
Electrical conductivity of the given aqueous solutions depends on the degree of ionisation. Degree of ionisation is directly proportional to the acidic strength. Electron withdrawing groups (EWGs) increases the stability of the carboxylate ion by dispersing the negative charge through resonance effect on the conjugate while electron donating groups (EDGs) decreases the stability of the carboxylate ion by intensifying the negative charge.

The correct order of acidic strength and electrical conductivity is as follows:
{tex}HC\underset AOOH\;>\;PhC\underset COOH\;>\;CH_3\underset BCOOH{/tex}

questions.question: Which one of the following graphs between molar conductivity ($\wedge_m$) versus $\sqrt C$ is correct?
questions.question_raw: Which one of the following graphs between molar conductivity ({tex}\wedge_m{/tex}) versus {tex}\sqrt C{/tex} is correct?
questions.explanation:
NaCl and KCI are strong electrolytes. So, the study of their molar conductances {tex}\left(\lambda_{\mathrm{m}}\right){/tex} can be experimentally verified by Debye-Huckel Onsagar equation,
{tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{c}=\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{0}-B \sqrt{C}{/tex}
{tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{c}{/tex} = molar conductance at concentration.
{tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{0}{/tex} = molar conductance at infinite dilution, i.e. C {tex}\to{/tex} 0
B = Debye-Huckel Onsagar constant.
For (both NaCl and KCI) a strong binary electrolyte like AB, the nature of the plot of {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}} v s \sqrt{C}{/tex} will be

Size of {tex}\mathrm{Na}^{\oplus}{/tex} is being smaller than {tex}\mathbf{K}^{\oplus}{/tex} and {tex}\mathrm{Na}^{\oplus}{/tex} will remain in the more hydrated state, i.e. larger sized in aqueous solution. As a result, ionic mobility as well as ionic conductance of {tex}\mathrm{Na}^{\oplus}{/tex} (or NaCl as {tex}\mathrm{Cl}^{\ominus}{/tex} is common to NaCl and KCl) will be lower than {tex}\mathbf{K}^{\oplus}{/tex} (or KCl). Thus, the plot of {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}} vs \sqrt{C}{/tex} for NaCl and KCl is as follows:

questions.question:
Consider the statements S1 and S2 :
S1 : Conductivity always increases with decrease in the concentration of electrolyte.
S2 : Molar conductivity always increases with decrease in the concentration of electrolyte.
The correct option among the following is
questions.question_raw:
Consider the statements S1 and S2 :
S1 : Conductivity always increases with decrease in the concentration of electrolyte.
S2 : Molar conductivity always increases with decrease in the concentration of electrolyte.
The correct option among the following is

options.text: S1 is correct and S2 is wrong
Both S1 and S2 are correct
S1 is wrong and S2 is correct
Both Si and S2 are wrong

questions.explanation:
The explanation of statements (S1 and S2) are as follows:
In conductivity cell, conductivity (k) is equal to the sum of ionic conductances (c), of an electrolytic solution present is unit volume of the solution enclosed by two electrodes of unit area (a {tex}\neq{/tex} 1) separated by a unit length (l = 1)
{tex}\kappa=c \times \frac{l}{a} \Rightarrow{/tex} k = c when l = 1, a = 1
So, with a decrease in the concentration of electrolyte, the number of ions in the given unit volume also decreases, i.e. k [conductivity] also decreases.
Thus, statement S1 is wrong. S2 : Molar conductivity {tex}\left(\lambda_{m}\right){/tex} is defined as the conducting power of all the ions present in a solution containing 1 mole of an electrolyte.
{tex}\lambda_{m}=\kappa \times V_{\mathrm{mL}}=\kappa \times \frac{1000}{M}{/tex}
where VmL = volume in mL containing 1 mole of electrolyte
m = molar concentration (mol/L)
So, in a conductivity cell {tex}\lambda_{m} \propto \frac{1}{M}{/tex} i.e. molar conductivity increases with a decrease in the concentration (M) of electrolyte.
Thus, statement S2 is correct.

questions.question:
The standard Gibbs energy for the given cell reaction in kJ mol-1 at 298 K is
Zn (s) + Cu2+(aq) $\rightarrow$Zn2+(aq) + Cu (s),
E° = 2V at 298 K
(Faraday’s constant, F = 96000 C mol-1)
questions.question_raw:
The standard Gibbs energy for the given cell reaction in kJ mol-1 at 298 K is
Zn (s) + Cu2+(aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex}Zn2+(aq) + Cu (s),
E° = 2V at 298 K
(Faraday’s constant, F = 96000 C mol-1)
questions.explanation:
Given reaction is,
Zn + Cu2+ {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Zn2+ + Cu
Eocell = 2.0 V
F = 96000 C
n = 2
To find the value of {tex}\Delta G^{\circ}{/tex} (kJ mol), we use the formula
{tex}\Delta G^{\circ}=-n F E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\Delta G^{\circ}{/tex} = -2 {tex}\times{/tex} 96000 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = -384000 J/mol
In terms of kJ/mol, {tex}\Delta G^{\circ}=\frac{-384000}{1000}{/tex}= -384 kJ/mol

questions.question: $\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}$ for NaCl, HCI and NaA are 126.4, 425.9 and 100.5 S cm2 mol-1, respectively. If the conductivity of 0.001 M HA is 5 $\times$ 10-5 S cm-1, degree of dissociation of HA is
questions.question_raw: {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}{/tex} for NaCl, HCI and NaA are 126.4, 425.9 and 100.5 S cm2 mol-1, respectively. If the conductivity of 0.001 M HA is 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 S cm-1, degree of dissociation of HA is
questions.explanation:
According to Kohlrausch’s law, the molar conductivity of H^4 at infinite dilution is given as,
{tex}\begin{aligned} \Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}(\mathrm{HA})=\left[\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}\right)+\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{Cl}^{-}\right)\right [&\left[\Lambda_{m}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{Na}^{+}\right)+\Lambda_{m}^{\circ}\left(A^{-}\right)\right]-\left[\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{Na}^{+}\right)+\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{Cl}^{-}\right)\right] \end{aligned}{/tex}
= 425.9 + 100.5 - 126.4 = 400 S cm2 mol-1
Also, molar conductivity at given concentration is given as {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}=\frac{1000 \times \mathrm{k}}{M}{/tex}
Given, k = conductivity {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 S cm-1
M = Molarity {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}0.001 M
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex} \Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}=\frac{1000 \times 5 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{S} \mathrm{cm}^{-1}}{10^{-3} \mathrm{M}} {/tex} = 50 S cm2 mol-1
Therefore, degree of dissociation {tex}(\alpha){/tex}, of HA is,
{tex}\alpha=\frac{\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}}{\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}}=\frac{50 \mathrm{S} \mathrm{cm}^{2} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}}{400 \mathrm{S} \mathrm{cm}^{2} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}}{/tex} = 0.125

questions.question:
Given the equilibrium constant (Kc) of the reaction: 
Cu(s) + 2Ag+(aq) $\rightarrow$ Cu2+ (aq) + 2Ag(s) 
is 10 $\times$ 1015, calculate the $E_{cell}^{0}$ of this reaction at 298 K. $\left[2303 \frac{R T}{F} \text { at } 298 \mathrm{K}=0.059 \mathrm{V}\right]$
questions.question_raw:
Given the equilibrium constant (Kc) of the reaction: 
Cu(s) + 2Ag+(aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Cu2+ (aq) + 2Ag(s) 
is 10 {tex}\times{/tex} 1015, calculate the {tex}E_{cell}^{0}{/tex} of this reaction at 298 K. {tex}\left[2303 \frac{R T}{F} \text { at } 298 \mathrm{K}=0.059 \mathrm{V}\right]{/tex}
questions.explanation:
According to Nernst equation
{tex}E_{\text {cell }}=E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}-\frac{2.303 R T}{n F} \log Q{/tex}
Given, {tex}\frac{2.303 R T}{F}{/tex} = 0.059 V
{tex}\therefore \quad E_{\text {cell }}=E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}-\frac{0.059}{n} \log Q{/tex}
At equilibrium, Ecell = 0
{tex}E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}=\frac{0.059}{n}{/tex} log Kc
For the given reaction, n = 2
Also, Kc = 10 {tex}\times{/tex} 1015 [given]
{tex}\therefore \quad E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}=\frac{0.059}{2}{/tex} log (10 {tex}\times{/tex} 1015) = 0.472V {tex}\approx{/tex} 0.473V

questions.question:
In the cell,
Pt(s) |H2 (g,1 bar) | HCl | (aq)| AgCl(s)| Ag(s)|Pt(s)
the cell potential is 0.92 V when a 10-6 molal HCl solution is used. The standard electrode potential of (AgCl/ Ag,Cl-) electrode is $\left\{\text { Given, } \frac{2.303 R T}{F}=0.06 \mathrm{V} \text { at } 298 \mathrm{K}\right\}$
questions.question_raw:
In the cell,
Pt(s) |H2 (g,1 bar) | HCl | (aq)| AgCl(s)| Ag(s)|Pt(s)
the cell potential is 0.92 V when a 10-6 molal HCl solution is used. The standard electrode potential of (AgCl/ Ag,Cl-) electrode is {tex}\left\{\text { Given, } \frac{2.303 R T}{F}=0.06 \mathrm{V} \text { at } 298 \mathrm{K}\right\}{/tex}
questions.explanation:
It is an electrochemical cell. The overall cell reaction can be written, as
{tex}\underset{(1bar)}{H_2}(g)\;+\;2AgCl(s)\;\rightarrow\underset{(10^{-6}M)}{2HCl(aq})\;+\;2Ag(s){/tex}

  1. According to Nernst equation,
    {tex}E_{\text {cell }}=\left(E_{\text {cathode }}^{c}-E_{\text {anode }}^{\circ}\right)-\frac{2303 \times R T}{n \times F} \log \frac{[\mathrm{HCl}]^{2}[\mathrm{Ag}]^{2}}{p_{\mathrm{H}_{2}}[\mathrm{AgCl}]^{2}}{/tex}
    Here, (i) {tex}E_{\mathrm{c}}^{\circ}=E_{\mathrm{AgCl} / \mathrm{Ag}, \mathrm{Cl}^{-}}^{\circ}=E_{\mathrm{cathode}}^{\circ}{/tex}
  2. {tex}E_{\text {anode }}^{\circ}=E_{2 \mathrm{H}^{+} / \mathrm{H}_{2}}^{\circ}{/tex} = 0.00 V
    (Standard hydrogen electrode)
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 0.92 = ({tex}E_{\mathrm{c}}^{\circ}{/tex} - 0) - 0.06 {tex}\times{/tex} log {tex}\frac{\left(10^{-6}\right)^{2} \times 1^{2}}{1 \times 1^{2}}{/tex}
    = {tex}E_{\mathrm{c}}^{\circ}{/tex} + 0.06 {tex}\times{/tex} 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 2
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}E_{\mathrm{c}}^{\circ}{/tex} = 0.92 - 0.72 = 0.20 V

questions.question:
If the standard electrode potential for a cell is 2 V at 300 K, the equilibrium constant (K) for the reaction,
Zn(s) + Cu2+(aq) $\rightleftharpoons$ Zn2+(aq) + Cu(s)
at 300 K is approximately
(R = 8 JK1 mol-1, F = 96000 C mol-1)
questions.question_raw:
If the standard electrode potential for a cell is 2 V at 300 K, the equilibrium constant (K) for the reaction,
Zn(s) + Cu2+(aq) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} Zn2+(aq) + Cu(s)
at 300 K is approximately
(R = 8 JK1 mol-1, F = 96000 C mol-1)
questions.explanation:
The relationship between standard electrode potential (E°) and equilibrium constant (K) of the cell reaction,
Zn(s) + Cu2+(aq) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex}Zn2+(aq) + Cu(s)
can be expressed as,
{tex}E^{\circ}=\frac{R T}{n F}{/tex} In K {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}K = {tex}e^{n F E^{\circ} / R T}{/tex}
Given, n = 2, F = 96000 C mol-1
Eo = 2V, R = 8 JK-1 mol-1
T = 300 K
{tex}K=e^{\frac{2 \times 96000 \times 2}{8 \times 300}}=e^{160}{/tex}

questions.question:
Given below are the half-cell reactions
Mn2+ + 2e- $\rightarrow$ Mn ; E° = - 1.18 eV
2 (Mn3+ + e- $\rightarrow$ Mn2+); E° = + 1.51 eV
The E° for 3Mn2+ $\rightarrow$ Mn + 2Mn3+ will be
questions.question_raw:
Given below are the half-cell reactions
Mn2+ + 2e- {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Mn ; E° = - 1.18 eV
2 (Mn3+ + e- {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Mn2+); E° = + 1.51 eV
The E° for 3Mn2+ {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Mn + 2Mn3+ will be

options.text: - 0.33 V; the reaction will not occur
- 2.69 V; the reaction will not occur

questions.explanation:
Standard electrode potential of reaction [E° ] can be calculated as {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}=E_{R}-E_{P}{/tex}
where, ER = SRP of reactant, EP = SRP of product
If {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}{/tex} = +ve, then reaction is spontaneous otherwise non-spontaneous.
{tex}Mn^{3+}\xrightarrow{E_1^o=1.51\;V}Mn^{2+}{/tex}
{tex}Mn^{2+}\xrightarrow{E_2^o=-1.18\;V}Mn{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} For Mn2+ disproportionation,
Eo = -1.51 V - 1.18 V = -2.69 V < 0
Thus, all reaction will not occur.

questions.question: The equivalent conductance of NaCl at concentration C and at infinite dilution are $\lambda$c and $\lambda_\infty$ relationship. The correct relationship between $\lambda$c and $\lambda_\infty$ is given as (where, the constant B is positive)

The correct relationship between {tex}\lambda{/tex}c and {tex}\lambda_\infty{/tex} is given as (where, the constant B is positive)
questions.explanation:
According to Debye Huckel Onsager equation,
{tex}\lambda{/tex}c = {tex}\lambda_\infty{/tex} - (B){tex}\sqrt C{/tex}
where, {tex}\lambda{/tex}c = limiting equivalent conductivity at concentration C
{tex}\lambda_\infty{/tex} = limiting equivalent conductivity at infinite dilution
C = concentration

questions.question: Resistance of 0.2 M solution of an electrolyte is 50 $\Omega$ . The specific conductance of the solution of 0.5 M solution of same electrolyte is 1.4 S m-1 and resistance of same solution of the same electrolyte is 280 $\Omega$ . The molar conductivity of 0.5 M solution of the electrolyte in Sm2mol-1 is
questions.question_raw: Resistance of 0.2 M solution of an electrolyte is 50 {tex}\Omega{/tex} . The specific conductance of the solution of 0.5 M solution of same electrolyte is 1.4 S m-1 and resistance of same solution of the same electrolyte is 280 {tex}\Omega{/tex} . The molar conductivity of 0.5 M solution of the electrolyte in Sm2mol-1 is
questions.explanation:
In order to solve the problem, calculate the value of cell constant of the first solution and then use this value of cell constant to calculate the value of k of second solution. Afterwards, finally calculate molar conductivity using value of k and m. For first solution,
k = 1.4 Sm-1, R = 50{tex}\Omega{/tex}, M = 0.2
Specific conductance {tex}(\kappa)=\frac{1}{R} \times \frac{l}{A}{/tex}
1.4 Sm-1 {tex}=\frac{1}{50} \times \frac{l}{A}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{l}{A}{/tex} = 50 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.4 m-1
For second solution, R = 280, {tex}\frac{l}{A}{/tex} = 50 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.4 m-1
k = {tex}\frac{1}{280}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 1.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 50 = {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex}
Now, molar conductivity
{tex}\lambda_{m}=\frac{\kappa}{1000 \times m}=\frac{1 / 4}{1000 \times 0.5}=\frac{1}{2000}{/tex}
= 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 Sm2 mol-1

E° (O2(g) + 2H2O + 4e-) $\rightarrow$ 4OH) = +0.40 V

Match E° of the rebox pair in Column I with the values given in Column II and select the correct answer using the code given below the lists.
 Column I
 Column II
 P. E°(Fe3+ / Fe2+ )
 1. -0.18 V
 Q. $E^{\circ}\left(4 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightleftharpoons 4 \mathrm{H}^{+}+4 \mathrm{OH}^{-}\right)$

 R. E°(Cu2+ + Cu $\rightarrow$ 2 Cu+)
 3. -0.04 V
 S. E°(Cr3+, Cr2+)

questions.question_raw:
The standard reduction potential data at 25 °C is given below.
E°(Fe3+ / Fe2+ ) = + 0.77 V; E°(Fe2 / Fe) = -0.44 V;
E°(Cu2+ / Cu) = + 0.34 V; E°(Cu+/Cu) = + 0.52 V;
E° (O2(g) + 4H+ + 4e-) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2H2O) = + 1.23 V;
E° (O2(g) + 2H2O + 4e-) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 4OH) = +0.40 V
E°(Cr3+ /Cr) = -0.74 V; E°(Cr2+ /Cr) = + 0.91 V
Match E° of the rebox pair in Column I with the values given in Column II and select the correct answer using the code given below the lists.

Column I Column II
P. E°(Fe3+ / Fe2+ ) 1. -0.18 V
Q. {tex}E^{\circ}\left(4 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightleftharpoons 4 \mathrm{H}^{+}+4 \mathrm{OH}^{-}\right){/tex} 2. -0.4 V
R. E°(Cu2+ + Cu {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2 Cu+) 3. -0.04 V
S. E°(Cr3+, Cr2+) 4. -0.83 V
questions.explanation:
(P) {tex}E_{3}^{\circ} \mathrm{Fe}^{3+} / \mathrm{Fe}{/tex}
Net reaction Fe3+ {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Fe is obtained from

Thus, P - (3)
Net reaction 4H2O {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 4H+ + 4OH- is obtained from

{tex}E_{3}^{\circ}=\frac{n_{1} E_{1}^{\circ}+n_{2} E^{\circ}_{2}}{n_{3}}=E_{1}^{\circ}+E_{2}^{\circ}{/tex}
= -1.23 + 0.40 = -0.83 V
Thus, Q - (4)
(R) Cu2+ + Cu {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2Cu+
For thus Eo of Cu2+ {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Cu+ is also required

{tex}E_{3}^{\circ}=\frac{n_{1} E_{1}^{\circ}+n_{2} E_{2}^{\circ}}{n_{3}}=\frac{2 \times 0.34+1 \times(-0.52)}{1}=0.16 \mathrm{V}{/tex}
Also,
 
Eo = -0.52 + 0.16 = - 0.36 V
Thus, (R) - (1)
(S) Cr3+ {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Cr2+ is obtained from

{tex}E_{3}^{\circ}=\frac{-0.74 \times 3+2 \times 0.91}{1}=-0.4 \mathrm{V}{/tex}
Thus, S = (2)
P - (3), Q - (4), R - (1), S - (2)

questions.question: AgNO3 (aqueous) was added to an aqueous KCI solution gradually and the conductivity of the solution was measured. The plot of conductance ($\Lambda$) versus the volume of AgNO3 is
questions.question_raw: AgNO3 (aqueous) was added to an aqueous KCI solution gradually and the conductivity of the solution was measured. The plot of conductance ({tex}\Lambda{/tex}) versus the volume of AgNO3 is
questions.explanation:
As AgNO3 is added to solution, KCl will be displaced according to following reaction
AgNO3 (aq) + KCl(aq) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} AgCl(s) + KNO3(aq)
For every mole of KCl displaced from solution, one mole of KNO3 comes in solution resulting in almost constant conductivity. As the end point is reached, added AgNO3 remain in solution increasing ionic concentration, hence conductivity increases.

questions.question: The correct order of equivalent conductance at infinite dilution of LiCl, NaCl and KCl is
questions.question_raw: The correct order of equivalent conductance at infinite dilution of LiCl, NaCl and KCl is
questions.explanation:
In LiCl, NaCl and KCl, anions are same.
Cations have same charge but different size. Smaller cations are more heavily hydrated in aqueous solution giving larger hydrated radius and thus smaller ionic speeds and equivalent conductance.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Equivalent conductance : KCl > NaCl > LiCl

questions.question: The oxidation number of the oxygen atom in H2O2 ion is
questions.question_raw: The oxidation number of the oxygen atom in H2O2 ion is
questions.explanation:
Oxygen almost always has an oxidation number of −2, except in peroxides.
A peroxide is a molecule that includes −O−O−, like hydrogen peroxide, HOOH.
In hydrogen peroxide, the overall charge is 0, so all the oxidation numbers must add up to 0. The oxidation number for hydrogen is always +1, so
HOOH = 0= 1 × 2 + X × 2
where X is the oxidation number for oxygen.
From this, we can deduce that  X = −1.

questions.question: The oxidatioin state of chromium in potassium dichromate is
questions.question_raw: The oxidatioin state of chromium in potassium dichromate is
questions.explanation:
Let  X, be the oxidation state of Cr in K2​Cr2​O7​.
 2(+1) + 2X + 7(−2)=0
2 + 2X−14= 0
2X=12X
=+6
Hence, the oxidation state of Cr in K2​Cr2​O7​ is +6.

questions.question:
In the balanced chemical reaction
$IO_3^ - + a{l^ - } + b{H^ + } \to C{H_2}{\text{O}} + d{l_2}$
a, b, c and d respectively corresponds to
questions.question_raw:
In the balanced chemical reaction
{tex}IO_3^ -  + a{l^ - } + b{H^ + } \to C{H_2}{\text{O}} + d{l_2}{/tex}
a, b, c and d respectively corresponds to
questions.explanation:
{tex}\mathrm{IO}_{3}^{-}+a \mathrm{I}^{-}+b \mathrm{H}^{+} \rightarrow c \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+d \mathrm{I}_{2}{/tex}
{tex}Step 1: I^{-} \rightarrow I_{2} (oxidation){/tex}
{tex}{IO}_{3}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{I}_{2} (reduction){/tex}
Step 2: {tex}2 I O_{3}^{-}+12 H^{+} \rightarrow I_{2}+6 H_{2} O{/tex}
Step 3: {tex}2IO_{3}^{-}+12 H^{+}+10 e^{-} \rightarrow I_{2}+6 H_{2} O{/tex}
{tex}2 \mathrm{I}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{I}_{2}+2 e^{-}{/tex}
Step 4: {tex}2IO_{3}^{-}+12 H^{+}+10 e^{-} \rightarrow I_{2}+6 H_{2} O{/tex}
{tex}\left[2 I^{-} \rightarrow I_{2}+2 e^{-}\right] 5{/tex}
Step 5:{tex} 2IO_{3}^{-}+10 I^{-}+12 H^{+} \rightarrow 6 I_{2}+6 H_{2} O{/tex}
{tex}IO_{3}^{-}+5 I^{-}+6 H^{+} \rightarrow 3 I_{2}+3 H_{2} O{/tex}
On comparing, a=5, b=6, c=3, d=3

questions.question: How many mL of $0.125{\text{ }}M{\text{ }}C{r^{3 + }}$ must be reacted with 12.00 mL of $0.200{\text{ }}M{\text{ }}MnO_4^ - $ if the redox products are $Cr{O_7}^{2 - }and{\text{ }}M{n^{2 + }}$?
questions.question_raw: How many mL of {tex}0.125{\text{ }}M{\text{ }}C{r^{3 + }}{/tex} must be reacted with 12.00 mL of {tex}0.200{\text{ }}M{\text{ }}MnO_4^ - {/tex} if the redox products are {tex}Cr{O_7}^{2 - }and{\text{ }}M{n^{2 + }}{/tex}?
questions.explanation:
Equivalents of
{tex}C r^{3+}=3 \times \text { moles of } C r^{3+}{/tex}
{tex}Equivalents \ of \ {MnO}_{4}^{-} =5 \times moles \ of \ {MnO}_{4}^{-}{/tex}
{tex}Amount \ of\ C r^{3+}=0.125 \times V milli \ mol{/tex}
{tex}=0.125 \times V \times 3 \text { miliequiv. }{/tex}
{tex}Amount \ of \ {MnO}_{4}^{-}=0.200 \times 12.00 \times 5 milliequiv{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad 0.125 \times V \times 3=0.200 \times 12.00 \times 5{/tex}
V = 32.0mL

questions.question: What is the oxidation state of sulphur in $N{a_2}{S_4}{O_6}$?
questions.question_raw: What is the oxidation state of sulphur in {tex}N{a_2}{S_4}{O_6}{/tex}?
questions.explanation:
Two S atoms namely S2 and S3 are joined together and with two other S atoms named S1 and S4.
Therefore their oxidation state will be zero. S atoms namely S1 and S4 have oxidation number +5 each.
Hence resultant oxidation number of sulphur in Na2S4O6 :
(0 + 0 + 5 + 5) / 4 = 2.5
So, oxidation state of  S2 and S3 = 0
and oxidation state of  S1 and S4 = +5 each
So, resultant O.N. of S = 2.5

If x is the oxidation number of Os, then
x + 4(-2) = 0 
gives, x = +8

50 mL of 0.1 M solution of a salt reacted with 25 mL of 0.1 solutions of sodium sulphite. The half reaction for the oxidation of sulphate ion is:
{tex}\mathrm{SO}_{3}^{2-}{/tex}(aq) + H2O(l) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{SO}_{3}^{2-}{/tex} (aq) + 2H+(aq) + 2e
If the oxidation number of metal in the salt was +3, what would be the new oxidation number of metal?
questions.explanation:
meq. of sodium sulphate = meq. of salt
25 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 50 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.1 {tex}\times{/tex} n
{tex}\therefore{/tex} n = 1 (where n represents valence factor for metal involving no. of electrons gained)
Thus M3+ + e {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} M2+

questions.explanation: 2N5+ {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} (N+)2 + 8e. Thus 2 mole of HNO3 will give one mole of N2O.

questions.question: How many gram of I2 are present in a solution which requires 40 mL of 0.11 N N2S2O3 to react with it, S2$\mathrm{O}_{3}^{2-}$ + I2 $\rightarrow$ S2$\mathrm{O}_{6}^{2-}$ + 2I-?

meq. of I2 = meq. of Na2S2O3
{tex}\therefore \ \frac{w}{\frac {254} 2} \times 1000{/tex} = 40 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.11
{tex}w_{1_{2}}{/tex} = 0.558 g

questions.question:
25 mL of 0.50 M H2O2 solution is added to 50 mL of 0.20 M KMnO4 in acid solution. Which of the following statements are true?
 0.010 mole of oxygen is liberated
 0.005 mole of KMnO4 are left
 0.030 g atom of oxygen gas is evolved
 0.0025 mole H2O2 does not react with KMnO4
questions.question_raw:
25 mL of 0.50 M H2O2 solution is added to 50 mL of 0.20 M KMnO4 in acid solution. Which of the following statements are true?

  1. 0.010 mole of oxygen is liberated
  2. 0.005 mole of KMnO4 are left
  3. 0.030 g atom of oxygen gas is evolved
  4. 0.0025 mole H2O2 does not react with KMnO4
questions.explanation:
Mn7+ + 5e {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Mn2+
(0-1)2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{O}_{2}^{0}{/tex} + 2e
meq. of H2O2 = 25 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 25;
meq. of KMnO4 = 50 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 = 50;
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 25 meq. or 5 millimole of KMnO4 are left

questions.question: What volume of O2 measured at standard conditions will be formed by the action of 100 mL of 0.5 N KMnO4 on hydrogen peroxide in an acid solution? The skeleton equaion for the reaction is, KMnO4 + H2SO4 + H2O2 $\rightarrow$ KHSO4 + MnSO4 + H2O + O2
questions.question_raw: What volume of O2 measured at standard conditions will be formed by the action of 100 mL of 0.5 N KMnO4 on hydrogen peroxide in an acid solution? The skeleton equaion for the reaction is, KMnO4 + H2SO4 + H2O2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} KHSO4 + MnSO4 + H2O + O2
questions.explanation:
meq. of O2 = meq. of KMnO4 = 100 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.5
{tex}\frac{w}{8} \times{/tex} 1000 = 50
{tex}\therefore \ w_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}{/tex} = 0.4 g
{tex}\therefore \ V_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}=\frac{22.4 \ \times \ 0.4}{32}{/tex} = 0.28 litre

questions.question: When the ion Cr2$O_{7}^{2-}$ acts as an oxidant in acidic aqueous solution the ion Cr3+ is formed. How many mole of Sn2+ would be oxidised to Sn4+ by one mole of Cr2$O_{7}^{2-}$ ions?
questions.question_raw: When the ion Cr2{tex}O_{7}^{2-}{/tex} acts as an oxidant in acidic aqueous solution the ion Cr3+ is formed. How many mole of Sn2+ would be oxidised to Sn4+ by one mole of Cr2{tex}O_{7}^{2-}{/tex} ions?

Mn7+ + 5e {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Mn2+] {tex}\times{/tex} 6

questions.question: A 0.518 g sample of limestone is dissolved in HCI and then the calcium is precipitated as CaC2O4. After filtering and washing the precipitate, it requires 40.0 mL of 0.250 N acidified solution of KMnO4 to titrate it as, Mn$O_4^-$ + H+ + C2$O_4^{2-}$ $\rightarrow$ Mn2+ + CO2 + 2H2O. The percentage of CaO in the sample is:
questions.question_raw: A 0.518 g sample of limestone is dissolved in HCI and then the calcium is precipitated as CaC2O4. After filtering and washing the precipitate, it requires 40.0 mL of 0.250 N acidified solution of KMnO4 to titrate it as, Mn{tex}O_4^-{/tex} + H+ + C2{tex}O_4^{2-}{/tex} {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Mn2+ + CO2 + 2H2O. The percentage of CaO in the sample is:
questions.explanation:
meq. of limestone = meq. of CaC2O4 = meq. of KMnO4 = meq. of CaO
{tex}\therefore \quad 40 \times 0.250=\frac{w}{\frac {56} 2} \times 1000{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} wCaO = 0.28
{tex}\therefore{/tex} percent of CaO = {tex}\frac{0.28 \ \times \ 100}{0.518}{/tex} = 54%

questions.question: Hydrogen peroxide in aqueous solution decomposes on warming to give oxygen according to the equation 2H2O2(aq) $\rightarrow$ 2H2O(l) + O2(g) under conditions where one mole of gas occupies 24 dm3, 100 cm3 of XM solution of H2O2 produces 3 dm3 of O2. Thus X is:
questions.question_raw: Hydrogen peroxide in aqueous solution decomposes on warming to give oxygen according to the equation 2H2O2(aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2H2O(l) + O2(g) under conditions where one mole of gas occupies 24 dm3, 100 cm3 of XM solution of H2O2 produces 3 dm3 of O2. Thus X is:
questions.explanation:
Mole of O2 formed = {tex}\frac{3}{24}=\frac{1}{8}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Mole of {tex}\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}=\frac{1}{8} \times 2=\frac{1}{4}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 100 {tex}\times{/tex} X = {tex}\frac{1}{4} \times 1000{/tex} (m mole = m {tex}\times{/tex} V)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} X = 2.5

questions.question: 8 g of sulphur are burnt to form SO2 which is oxidised by Cl2 water. The solution is treated with BaCl2 solution. The mole of BaSO4 precipitated is:
questions.question_raw: 8 g of sulphur are burnt to form SO2 which is oxidised by Cl2 water. The solution is treated with BaCl2 solution. The mole of BaSO4 precipitated is:
questions.explanation:
Mass of SO2 formed = {tex}\frac{64 \ \times \ 8}{32}{/tex} = 16 g
Now S4+ {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} S6+ + 2e
mole of {tex}{SO}_{4}^{2-}{/tex} formed = {tex}\frac{16}{96}=\frac{1}{6}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} mole of BaSO4 = {tex}\frac{1}{6}{/tex} = 0.33

VO2+ is oxovanadium (iv) ion

a = +4

questions.explanation:
Average oxidation no. of M = {tex}+\frac{200}{98}{/tex} (lies between 2 and 3)
Let % of M2+ be a and of M3+ be b
or {tex}\frac{2 \ \times\ a \ + \ (100 \ - \ a) \ \times \ 3}{100}{/tex} = 2.04 ({tex}\because{/tex} a + b = 100)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 2a + 300 - 3a = {tex}\frac{200}{98}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} +a = 300 - 2.04 {tex}\times{/tex} 100
= 300 - 204 = 96
Thus M2+ = 96%
M2+ = 4%

questions.question:

K2Cr2O7 + 14HCI $\rightarrow$ 2KCI + 2CrCl3 + 3CI2
questions.question_raw:
Equivalent mass of HCl in the reactions is:
K2Cr2O7 + 14HCI {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2KCI + 2CrCl3 + 3CI2
questions.explanation:
14 mole HCI = 3 mole Cl2 = 6 eq. Cl2 [2CI- {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} CI2 + 2e {tex}\therefore{/tex} n factor for CI2 = 2]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 1 mole HCI = {tex}\frac 37{/tex}eq.CI2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} EHCI = {tex}\frac{M}{\frac 3 7}=\frac{7 \ \times \ M}{3}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{7 \ \times \ 36.5}{3}{/tex} = 85.16

questions.question:

3CI2 + 6NaOH $\rightarrow$ 5NaCl + NaCIO3 + 3H2O is:
questions.question_raw:

3CI2 + 6NaOH {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 5NaCl + NaCIO3 + 3H2O is:
questions.explanation:
2e + (Cl0)2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2CI-
(Cl0)2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2CI5+ + 10e
3 mole Cl2 = 5 mole NaCl
= 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 eq. NaCl
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 1 mole Cl2 = {tex}\frac {10}3{/tex}eq. NaCl = {tex}\frac {10}3{/tex}eq. Cl2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} n-factor for Cl2 = {tex}\frac {10}3{/tex}
or 3 mole Cl2 = 1 mole NaCIO3
= 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 eq. NaCIO3
or 1 mole Cl2 = {tex}\frac {10}3{/tex}eq. NaCIO3 = {tex}\frac {10}3{/tex} eq. Cl2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Eq. mass of Cl2 = {tex}\frac{17}{\frac {10} 3}=\frac{213}{10}{/tex} = 21.3

questions.question:
H2O2 + O3 $\rightarrow$ H2O + 2O2
 H2O2 + Ag2O $\rightarrow$ 2Ag + H2O + O2
 Role of hydrogen peroxide in the above reactions is respectively:
 oxidizing agent in (a) and reducing agent in (b)
 reducing agent in (a) and oxidizing agent in (b)
 reducing agent in (a) and (b)
 oxidizing agent in (a) and (b)
questions.question_raw:
1. H2O2 + O3 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} H2O + 2O2
  2. H2O2 + Ag2O {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2Ag + H2O + O2
    Role of hydrogen peroxide in the above reactions is respectively:
    1. oxidizing agent in (a) and reducing agent in (b)
    2. reducing agent in (a) and oxidizing agent in (b)
    3. reducing agent in (a) and (b)
    4. oxidizing agent in (a) and (b)
questions.explanation:
1. H2O2 is oxidised and thus reductant
    (O-1)2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} (O0)2 + 2e
  2. Ag2O is reduced and thus H2O2 is reductant
    Ag+ + e {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Ag
    (O-1)2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} (O0)2 + 2e

questions.question:
1 mole of CrO5 reacts with H2SO4 and liberates oxygen
CrO5 + H2SO4 $\rightarrow$ Cr,(SO4)3 + H2O + O2
The mole of O2 liberated are:
questions.question_raw:
1 mole of CrO5 reacts with H2SO4 and liberates oxygen
CrO5 + H2SO4 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Cr,(SO4)3 + H2O + O2
The mole of O2 liberated are:
questions.explanation:
On babmcing other atoms
16CrO5 + 24H2SO4 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 8Cr2(SO4)3 + 24H2O + 28O2
or 2CrO5 + 3H2SO4 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Cr2(SO4)3 + 3H2O + {tex}\frac{7}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}{/tex}
Thus, 2 mole CrO5 = {tex} \frac{7}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 1 mole CrO5 = {tex} \frac{7}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}{/tex}

questions.question:
Thiosulphate reacts differently with iodine and bromine in the reactions given below:
$2 \mathrm{S}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}^{2-}+\mathrm{I}_{2}$ $\rightarrow \mathrm{S}_{4} \mathrm{O}_{6}^{2-}+2 \mathrm{I}^-$
$\mathrm{S}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}^{2-}$ + 2Br2 + 5H2O $\rightarrow 2 \mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}$ + 2Br- + 10H+
Which of the following statements justifies the above dual behavior of thiosulphate?
questions.question_raw:
Thiosulphate reacts differently with iodine and bromine in the reactions given below:
{tex}2 \mathrm{S}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}^{2-}+\mathrm{I}_{2}{/tex} {tex}\rightarrow \mathrm{S}_{4} \mathrm{O}_{6}^{2-}+2 \mathrm{I}^-{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{S}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}^{2-}{/tex} + 2Br2 + 5H2O {tex}\rightarrow 2 \mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}{/tex} + 2Br- + 10H+
Which of the following statements justifies the above dual behavior of thiosulphate?

options.text: Bromine is a weaker oxidant than iodine.
Thiosulphate undergoes oxidation by bromine and reduction by iodine in these reactions.
Bromine is a stronger oxidant than iodine.
Bromine undergoes oxidation and iodine undergoes reduction in these reactions.

questions.explanation:
{tex}2 \mathrm{S}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}^{2-}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{I}_{2}^{0}(\mathrm{s}){/tex} {tex}\rightarrow{2.5} \mathrm{S}_{4} \mathrm{O}_{6}^{2-}{/tex} (aq) + 2I- (aq)
{tex}\mathrm{S}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}^{2-}{/tex} (aq) + 2Br2 (I) + 5H2O(I) {tex}\rightarrow 2 \mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-} {/tex} (aq) + 4Br- (aq) + 10H+ (aq)
Bromine being stronger oxidizing agent than I2, therefore it oxidises S of {tex}\mathrm{S}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}^{2-}{/tex} to higher oxidation state +6 {tex} \mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-} {/tex} whereas I2 oxidises it only into  {tex} \mathrm{S}_{4} \mathrm{O}_{6}^{2-}{/tex}ion.

questions.question: Identify disproportionation reaction.
questions.question_raw: Identify disproportionation reaction.
questions.explanation:
Reactions in which the same substance is oxidized as well as reduced are called disproportionation reactions. Writing the O.N. of each element above its symbol in the given reactions,
{tex}{\mathop {{\rm{2NO}}}\limits^{ + 4 - 2} {_{\rm{2}}}}{\rm{ + 2}}\mathop {\rm{O}}\limits^{ - 2} {\rm{H}}\mathop {^{\rm{ - }}}\limits^{ + 1} {/tex}{tex} \to \mathop {\rm{NO_2}}\limits^{ + 3-2} {\rm{}}\mathop {\mathop {\rm{ + }}\limits_{^{\rm{}}} }\limits^{ } {\rm{ }}\mathop {\rm{NO_3}}\limits^{ + 5-2} {\rm{}}\mathop {\mathop {\rm{ +}}\limits_{^{\rm{}}} }\limits^{ } {\rm{ }}{\mathop {\rm{H}}\limits^{ + 1} {_2}}\mathop {\rm{O}}\limits^{ - 2} {/tex}
Thus, in reaction, N is both oxidized as well as reduced since the O.N. N increases from (+4 state) in NO2 to (+5 state) in NO-3 and decreases from (+4 state) in NO2 to (+3 state) in NO-2.

questions.question:
What is the change in oxidation number of carbon in the following reaction?
CH4(g) + 4Cl2(g) $\to$ CCl4(I) + 4HCl(g)
questions.question_raw:
What is the change in oxidation number of carbon in the following reaction?
CH4(g) + 4Cl2(g) {tex}\to{/tex} CCl4(I) + 4HCl(g)
questions.explanation:
{tex}\mathop C\limits^{ - 4} {H_4}(g) + 4C{l_2}(g) \to \mathop C\limits^{ + 4} C{l_4}(I) + 4HCl(g){/tex}
The oxidation state of H and Cl are +1 and -1 respectively. Therefore the oxidation state of C in methane is −4 and that in carbon tetrachloride is +4.

questions.question_raw: In balancing the half reaction, {tex}S_2O_3^{2-} \longrightarrow{/tex} S(s), the number of electrons that must be added is:

{tex}\underset{+2}{S_2O_3^{2-}} \longrightarrow \underset{0}S(s){/tex}

questions.question: The oxidation state of I in $H_4IO_6^-$ is:
questions.question_raw: The oxidation state of I in {tex}H_4IO_6^-{/tex} is:
questions.explanation:
Let oxidation state of I in {tex}H_4IO_6^-{/tex}​ is x.
Since the overall charge on the complex is −1, the sum of oxidation states of all elements in it should be equal to −1.
Therefore, 4 + x − 12 = −1 (Here, oxidation state of H is +1 and oxygen is -2) or, x = 7

questions.question: The oxidation number of Pt in [Pt(C2H4)Cl3]- is:
questions.question_raw: The oxidation number of Pt in [Pt(C2H4)Cl3]- is:
questions.explanation:
Let x be the oxidation state of Pt in [Pt(C2​H4​)Cl3​]−.
Since the overall charge on the complex is −1, the sum of oxidation states of all elements in it should be equal to −1.
Therefore, x + 0 − 3 = −1
or, x = +2

questions.question: Equivalent mass of H3PO2 (Molar mass = 66) when it disproportionate into PH3 and H3PO3 is:
questions.question_raw: Equivalent mass of H3PO2 (Molar mass = 66) when it disproportionate into PH3 and H3PO3 is:
questions.explanation:
Reaction: {tex}\underset{+1}{H_3​PO_2}\longrightarrow \underset{-3}PH_3​+\underset{+3}{H_3​PO_3​}{/tex}
So, the equivalent mass (if it goes to PH3​) of H3​PO3 ​= {tex}\frac{{M}}{4}=\frac{{6 6}}{4}{/tex} = 16.5
the equivalent mass (if it goes to H3​PO3​) of H3​PO3 = {tex}\frac{{M}}{{2}}=\frac{{6 6}}{{2}}{/tex} ​= 33
So, total equivalent mass = 16.5 + 33 = 49.5

questions.question: The brown ring complex [Fe(H2O)5 NO+]SO4 has ox. no. of Fe:
questions.question_raw: The brown ring complex [Fe(H2O)5 NO+]SO4 has ox. no. of Fe:
questions.explanation:
NO in brown ring complex has +1 oxidation state.
Let oxidation state of Fe be x.
Since the overall charge on the coordination sphere, [Fe(H2​O)5​NO+]  is +2, the sum of oxidation states of all elements in it should be equal to +2.
Therefore, x + 1 = +2
or, x = +1 
Hence, the oxidation state of iron in the brown ring complex is +1.

questions.question: 2 mole of FeSO4 in acid medium are oxidized by x mole of KMnO4, whereas 2 mole of FeC2O4 in acid medium are oxidized by y mole of KMnO4. The ratio of x and y is:
questions.question_raw: 2 mole of FeSO4 in acid medium are oxidized by x mole of KMnO4, whereas 2 mole of FeC2O4 in acid medium are oxidized by y mole of KMnO4. The ratio of x and y is:
questions.explanation:
The balanced reactions are given below:
2KMnO4 ​+ 10FeSO4 ​+ 8H2​SO4 ​{tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 5Fe2​(SO4​)3 ​+ K2​SO4 ​+ 2MnSO4 ​+ 8H2​O and
10FeC2​O4 ​+ 6KMnO4 ​+ 24H2​SO4 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 3K2​SO4 ​+ 6MnSO4 ​+ 5Fe2​(SO4​)3 ​+ 24H2​O + 20CO2​
So, from the reactions, we get {tex}\frac{x}{y}=\frac{4}{10} \times \frac{10}{12}=\frac 13{/tex}

questions.question: Oxidation state of nitrogen is incorrectly given for:
questions.question_raw: Oxidation state of nitrogen is incorrectly given for:
questions.explanation:
1. [Co(NH3​)5​Cl]Cl​2
    Let oxidation number of N = x
    Oxidation number of NH3​ = 0
    x + 1(3) = 0
    x = −3 is correct.
  2. NH2​OH
    Let oxidation number of N = x
    Oxidation number of H = +1
    Oxidation number of O = −2
    x + 3(+1) + (−2) = 0
    x = −1 is correct
  3. (N2​H5​)​2SO4​
    Let oxidation number of N = x
    Oxidation number of H = +1
    Oxidation number of SO4​ = −2
    2(2(x) + 5(+1)) + (−2) = 0
    x = −2 is incorrect
  4. Mg3​N2
    Let oxidation number of N = x
    Oxidation number of Mg = +2
    2(x) + 3(+2) = 0
    x = −3 is correct

questions.question: Iodimetric titrations are usually performed in neutral or mildly alkaline (pH = 8) or weakly acidic solution. Which statement is not valid for this observation?
questions.question_raw: Iodimetric titrations are usually performed in neutral or mildly alkaline (pH = 8) or weakly acidic solution. Which statement is not valid for this observation?

options.text: In strong acidic solutions starch used to detect the end point tends to hydrolyse or decomposed.
Reducing power of reducing agent is increased in strong acidic medium.
In strong alkaline solution I2 disproportionate to I- and IO-.
I- produced during titration tends to be oxidized by dissolved oxygen in acidic medium.

questions.explanation: Reducing power of the reducing agent increases in an acidic medium. This observation when if the pH is too acidic. The remaining options are observed during the titration in a weakly acidic solution.

questions.question: The end point of iodometric titrations is detected by adding starch just near the end point and not at the beginning of titration. Which statement is not valid for this fact?
questions.question_raw: The end point of iodometric titrations is detected by adding starch just near the end point and not at the beginning of titration. Which statement is not valid for this fact?

options.text: Iodometric titrations are made in neutral medium where starch is neither hydrolysed nor decomposed.
Due to the slow decomposition of complex a diffuse endpoint is obtained.
Iodometric titrations are made in a strong alkaline medium where starch is either hydrolyzed or decomposed.

questions.explanation:
Iodometry is based on the oxidation of iodide into iodine. Iodometry is used for determining the amount of oxidizing agents. The amount of oxidizing agent is determined by titration of iodine with thiosulfate. Starch is used as an indicator. The endpoint detection is based on the formation of the blue starch complex. The chemical reaction is as follows:
I2 ​+ Na2S2O3 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2NaI + Na2S2O4
This is the principle reaction that shows the reduction of iodine. Thus, the halogens, oxyhalogens, cupric ions, peroxides, sulfur dioxide in the food industry, etc. can be measured by this method. Iodometric titrations are completed in a weakly acidic medium due to the decomposition of thiosulphate. The pH of iodine solution should be < 8.5 because iodine disproportionates at basic pH.

questions.question: Among the following, identify the species with an atom in +6 oxidation state:
questions.question_raw: Among the following, identify the species with an atom in +6 oxidation state:
questions.explanation:
Let x be the oxidation state of Mn in {tex}MnO_4^−​{/tex}.
Since the overall charge on the complex is −1, the sum of oxidation states of all elements in it should be equal to −1.
Therefore, x + 4(−2) = −1
or, x = +7
Hence, the oxidation state of Mn in {tex}MnO_4^−​{/tex}​ is +7.
Similarly,
The oxidation state of Cr in [Cr(CN)6​]3−, Ni in {tex}NiF_6^{6−}{/tex}​ and Cr in CrO2​Cl2​ is +3, +4 and +6 respectively.
Hence, the species with an atom in +6 oxidation state is CrO2Cl2.

questions.explanation:
Consider the compound K4​[Ni(CN)4​]
Let oxidation state of Nickel be x
(1 {tex}\times{/tex} 4) + x + (4 {tex}\times{/tex} −1) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4 + x − 4 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 0
Hence, in the given compound, the oxidation state of Nickel is zero.

questions.question_raw:

questions.explanation:
Spectator ions are the ions which do not take part in the reaction.
{tex}Na+2HCl\longrightarrow 2NaCl+H_2{/tex}

Only for chloride ions, oxidation state doesn't change, so they are spectator here.

questions.question: Excess of KI reacts with CuSO4 solution and then Na2S2O3 solution is added to it. Which of the statements is incorrect in this reaction?
questions.question_raw: Excess of KI reacts with CuSO4 solution and then Na2S2O3 solution is added to it. Which of the statements is incorrect in this reaction?
questions.explanation:
Copper sulphate reacts with potassium iodide to form cuprous iodide and iodine.
2CuSO4 ​+ 4KI {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Cu2​I2 {tex}\downarrow{/tex} + I2 ​+ 2K2​SO4​
Thus, CuI2​ is not formed in this reaction.
The liberated iodine is titrated with sodium thiosulphate to form sodium tetrathionate.
2Na2​S2​O3 ​+ I2​ {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Na2​S4​O6​ + 2NaI
Iodine is reduced and sodium thiosulphate is oxidized.

questions.question: Which of the following changes represents a disproportionation reaction (s)?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following changes represents a disproportionation reaction (s)?

Disproportionation is a specific type of redox reaction in which a species is simultaneously reduced and oxidised to form two different products.
Cl2 + 2OH- {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} ClO- + Cl- + H2O
Cu2O + 2H+ {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Cu + Cu2+ + H2O
2HCuCl2 {tex}\xrightarrow {Dilution \ with \ water}{/tex} Cu + Cu2+ + 4Cl- + 2H+
So, they all are disproportional reactions.
